subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Architecture from a recognized university
Undergraduate studies also focused on architecture or a closely related field
Participated in studio-based courses emphasizing both design and technical skills
Completed coursework on building codes, construction methods, and sustainable design
Attended workshops on advanced modeling software during graduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Junior Architect
Works as part of a multidisciplinary design team in a large company
Involved in different stages of the architectural design process, from concept to detail development
Regularly communicates design concepts to colleagues and stakeholders
Assists senior architects in project documentation and client presentations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Employed in the architecture and architectural design sector
Focuses on building design for public, private, and institutional clients
Operates within the broader construction and built environment industry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works for a large design company with multiple departments
Company employs over 100 professionals including architects, engineers, and designers
Collaborative, multidisciplinary environment with frequent cross-team meetings
Office setup includes both open-plan workspaces and meeting rooms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company operates as an architectural design consultancy
Engages in projects ranging from large public buildings to small pavilions and private spaces
Participates in design competitions to secure new projects
Serves educational, governmental, and private sector clients

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a Junior Architect
Joined the current company shortly after completing graduate studies
Has worked on at least one major project for over a year
Gained initial professional experience through internships prior to current role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level to early-career professional
Reports to senior architects and project leads
Responsible for supporting key design and documentation tasks
Not yet in a management or supervisory position

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Develops architectural designs and digital models using CAD and 3D software
Refines project details including materials, structural layouts, and compliance with regulations
Prepares presentations and visualizations for internal reviews and client meetings
Participates in project meetings with internal teams and stakeholders
Assists with documentation required for design competitions or project proposals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60% of time on design development and modeling
20% of time dedicated to meetings and collaborative discussions
10% of time spent on preparing presentations and visualizations
Remaining 10% allocated to project documentation and administrative tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Makes independent decisions regarding model development and detailing within assigned scope
Selects appropriate modeling tools and techniques for specific design challenges
Major design directions and critical decisions are reviewed and approved by senior architects
Contributes suggestions during team discussions but final authority rests with project leads

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Building 3D models in SketchUp and AutoCAD feels intuitive due to strong software skills
Translating conceptual sketches into digital representations
Creating visual presentations using PowerPoint
Collaborating with team members during design brainstorming sessions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Navigating regulatory compliance for fire safety and earthquake resistance
Integrating complex building systems (e.g., HVAC, plumbing) within architectural constraints
Coordinating input from multiple departments and stakeholders
Refining detailed construction drawings to meet strict client and code requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Updating area calculations and space schedules in Excel
Checking design compliance against multiple regulatory checklists
Preparing basic renderings for early-stage presentations
Formatting and organizing documentation for submissions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Most enjoyable: conceptual design and creative modeling
Finds satisfaction in producing high-quality renderings for presentations
Tedious: repetitive documentation updates and manual compliance checks
Least engaging: formatting lengthy reports or meeting detailed administrative requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual compliance checks are time-consuming and error-prone
Interoperability issues between modeling and rendering software cause workflow delays
Frequent revisions required after stakeholder feedback extend project timelines
Gathering and integrating inputs from multiple departments can be inefficient

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment and proficiency in modeling tasks lead to increased productivity
Finds repetitive admin work less engaging, which can slow completion rates
Enjoyment peaks when creative autonomy is allowed within project guidelines
Skill development in new software tools correlates with higher engagement and output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Enthusiastic about exploring new design and modeling tools
Views technological learning as essential for career growth
Regularly seeks out tutorials and webinars on advanced software features
Open-minded about integrating AI-powered tools into workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self highly adaptable, especially with design software
Quickly picks up new features or platforms through hands-on experimentation
Able to transfer skills between similar modeling and rendering environments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time outside regular hours for learning if the tool enhances productivity
Has completed online courses on advanced modeling and rendering techniques
Participates in company-sponsored training sessions when available

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential time savings and improved design quality
Workload can sometimes limit time available for deep learning
Relevance to current projects is a key factor in prioritizing new tools
Peer recommendations can influence willingness to try new technologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers frequently share tips and resources for new design software
Management encourages adoption of technologies that streamline workflows
Team culture supports experimentation with emerging tools, including AI
Positive experiences shared by colleagues increase openness to tool adoption

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses AutoCAD and SketchUp daily for drafting and 3D modeling
Employs Rhino and Grasshopper for more complex parametric modeling tasks
Regularly uses V-Ray and Enscape for rendering high-quality visualizations
PowerPoint is used for preparing project presentations
Excel and Word are used for data management and documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: AutoCAD is essential for technical drawings and floor plans
SketchUp is the primary tool for quick conceptual 3D modeling
V-Ray and Enscape are indispensable for producing realistic renderings
Microsoft Office suite supports documentation and communication

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in standard architectural software used across the industry
Familiar with interoperability between CAD, BIM, and rendering platforms
Has basic exposure to BIM tools (e.g., Revit), though not a daily user
Keeps updated on new releases and features of core software packages

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally encounters compatibility issues transferring models from Rhino to SketchUp
Rendering plugins sometimes have version conflicts with core modeling software
Formatting inconsistencies arise when exporting between CAD and presentation tools
Manual data transfer is needed between modeling and compliance-checking processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI concepts such as machine learning, natural language processing, and generative design
Can explain the difference between rule-based and data-driven AI systems
Familiar with the application of AI in design tasks, such as image synthesis and plan generation
Keeps up with industry discussions about AI's role in architecture

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT for generating text documentation and design briefs
Has experimented with AI-powered rendering tools (e.g., DALL-E, Midjourney) for conceptual visuals
Occasionally uses AI-enhanced plugins within modeling software for efficiency
Explores generative design features available in Grasshopper and similar platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs weekly for drafting reports, summarizing meeting notes, and proposal writing
Uses AI image generators for early-stage design exploration and mood boards
Leverages AI tools to automate code compliance checks when possible
Employs AI for rapid generation of alternative design options during brainstorming

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used LLMs to streamline proposal writing, reducing preparation time by 30%
AI-generated renderings have impressed teams during internal reviews
Encountered inaccuracies in AI-generated code compliance reports, highlighting need for manual validation
Learned that AI suggestions are most reliable when coupled with domain expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company has provided informal workshops on using LLMs for documentation
Peers often share best practices and prompt templates for AI tools
Official documentation for AI tool usage is still limited
Encouraged to self-learn and experiment with new AI-powered features

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex design tasks
Often starts independently, then uses AI for refining output or checking work
Values iterative feedback loops between human creativity and AI suggestions
Finds AI most useful as an assistant rather than a sole decision-maker

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for automating routine or data-heavy tasks
Relies on AI as an advisor during the ideation and concept development stages
Occasionally treats AI as a co-worker when exploring multiple design alternatives

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to integrating AI-driven workflows that improve efficiency
Eager to pilot new AI features as they become available within core tools
Advocates for team adoption of proven AI solutions after successful trials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and generating text content
Finds command-based interfaces efficient for specific, repeatable actions in modeling software
Uses both interface types depending on the task and tool

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many design tasks require deep understanding of architectural principles and client needs
Tacit knowledge is critical for interpreting and applying building codes
Material selection and user experience considerations depend on domain expertise
AI outputs often require human review to ensure adherence to project vision

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine documentation and initial idea generation
Exercises caution with AI-generated compliance checks and technical details
Final decisions are always validated by experienced architects
Partial trust in AI outputs, with manual review as a safety net

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive, rule-based, or data-heavy tasks
Human oversight is essential for creative and critical design decisions
Sees value in a hybrid approach where AI accelerates workflow but humans ensure quality
Believes some tasks, such as client presentations, should remain primarily human-led

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for tasks with clear, objective criteria (e.g., code checking, rendering)
Feels threatened by automation that encroaches on creative or client-facing responsibilities
Sees automation as beneficial when it frees up time for higher-value design work
Prefers transparent AI processes with the option for manual override

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances technical skills by automating routine aspects, enabling focus on creativity
Worries that over-reliance on AI could erode foundational drafting or compliance-checking skills
Sees increased need for skills in AI tool management and prompt engineering
Values adaptability and lifelong learning as critical skills in the AI era

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing proficiency in crafting effective AI prompts for design and documentation tasks
Learning to evaluate and validate AI-generated outputs for reliability
Gaining responsibility for integrating AI workflows into project processes
Staying updated on the ethical implications of AI in design

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about AI automating routine documentation and compliance checks
Believes creative and client-facing aspects of architecture remain secure
Concerned that rapid AI adoption may reduce entry-level opportunities in the field
Sees AI as a tool for augmentation rather than total replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review and approval is required for all critical AI-generated documents
AI tools in use allow for user intervention and adjustment at every step
Project leads insist on a human sign-off before submission to clients or authorities
Company policy mandates double-checking AI outputs for compliance-sensitive work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is gradually formalizing guidelines for responsible AI use
Team discussions increasingly address best practices and ethical considerations of AI
Policy has shifted to encourage experimentation with AI tools for efficiency gains
Ongoing development of internal resources and training for AI integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will revolutionize the architectural design process by increasing efficiency
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to high-quality design resources
Recognizes risks of job displacement in routine or low-skill tasks
Optimistic that AI will lead to more innovative and sustainable building solutions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Advocates for transparency and explainability in all AI-assisted decisions
Worries about data privacy and intellectual property issues with AI-generated designs
Supports ethical guidelines to prevent bias in AI-driven design processes
Believes human oversight is essential to maintain accountability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that can automatically generate code-compliant floor plans
Wishes for real-time, integrated AI assistants within modeling software
Would benefit from AI that can instantly highlight design errors or regulatory conflicts
Seeks AI-driven tools for rapid iteration of design alternatives based on constraints

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates spending less time on manual drafting and more on creative problem-solving
Expects growing collaboration with AI in both design generation and project management
Believes architects will become curators and validators of AI-generated solutions
Foresees increased demand for AI literacy and integration skills in the profession

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement structured training programs for AI tool usage across teams
Establish clear guidelines and best practices for ethical AI deployment
Invest in development or acquisition of AI tools tailored to architectural workflows
Encourage a culture of experimentation and feedback for continuous AI improvement
